questions.stem: A nurse reviews orders for a patient admitted for an elective knee MRI with IV contrast scheduled for tomorrow. The patient takes metformin 1000 mg BID for type 2 diabetes. Which nursing action is MOST appropriate?

options.text: Administer metformin as scheduled since the procedure is elective
Hold metformin beginning the day of and for 48 hours after contrast administration, and notify the ordering provider
Switch the patient to insulin therapy permanently before the procedure
Administer metformin with the morning meal on the day of the procedure and hold only for 24 hours post-contrast

questions.rationale: Metformin is renally cleared. IV iodinated contrast can cause contrast-induced nephropathy (CIN), acutely impairing renal function. If renal function is reduced by CIN, metformin accumulates and raises the risk of lactic acidosis — a rare but potentially fatal complication. Standard protocol: hold metformin on the day of contrast administration and for 48 hours after, then re-assess renal function (creatinine/eGFR) before resuming. Permanent insulin switch is not necessary. Administering on the day of the procedure violates the safety protocol. The provider should be notified to confirm the hold order.

distractorRationales.a: Administer metformin as scheduled since the procedure is elective misses the clinical pattern: Pharmacology / Metformin Safety insulin points to Hold metformin beginning the day of and for 48 hours after contrast administration, and notify the, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen unstable glucose physiology.
distractorRationales.c: Switch the patient to insulin therapy permanently before the procedure misses the clinical pattern: Pharmacology / Metformin Safety insulin points to Hold metformin beginning the day of and for 48 hours after contrast administration, and notify the, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen unstable glucose physiology.
distractorRationales.d: Administer metformin with the morning meal on the day of the procedure and hold only for misses the clinical pattern: Pharmacology / Metformin Safety insulin points to Hold metformin beginning the day of and for 48 hours after contrast administration, and notify the, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen unstable glucose physiology.

questions.takeaway: Metformin + IV contrast: hold the day of and 48h after. Risk is lactic acidosis from metformin accumulation if contrast causes AKI. Check creatinine/eGFR before restarting. This applies to any iodinated contrast study.

questions.stem: A nurse receives a SBAR hand-off report. The oncoming nurse should be MOST concerned about which patient?

options.text: A 67-year-old with COPD whose O2 sat is 91% on 2L nasal cannula, which is their baseline
A 52-year-old with sepsis whose urine output has been 15 mL/hour for the past 3 hours
A 78-year-old post-hip replacement who refuses to ambulate and states 'I'm tired'
A 45-year-old on heparin infusion whose aPTT result is pending from 2 hours ago

questions.rationale: Urine output of 15 mL/hr for 3 consecutive hours (total 45 mL in 3 hours, target minimum 30 mL/hr) in a patient with sepsis signals inadequate renal perfusion and worsening organ dysfunction. This meets criteria for oliguria and may indicate septic shock progression or acute kidney injury, requiring immediate provider notification and likely fluid resuscitation. The COPD patient's 91% sat is their established baseline; the hip replacement refusal is important but not acutely threatening; the pending aPTT should be followed but is not immediately dangerous.

distractorRationales.a: A 67-year-old with COPD whose O2 sat is 91% on 2L nasal cannula, which is their misses the clinical pattern: Safety And Infection Control / Prioritization perfusion points to A 52-year-old with sepsis whose urine output has been 15 mL/hour for the past 3 hours, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen bleeding or anticoagulation injury.
distractorRationales.c: A 78-year-old post-hip replacement who refuses to ambulate and states 'I'm tired' misses the clinical pattern: Safety And Infection Control / Prioritization perfusion points to A 52-year-old with sepsis whose urine output has been 15 mL/hour for the past 3 hours, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen bleeding or anticoagulation injury.
distractorRationales.d: A 45-year-old on heparin infusion whose aPTT result is pending from 2 hours ago misses the clinical pattern: Safety And Infection Control / Prioritization perfusion points to A 52-year-old with sepsis whose urine output has been 15 mL/hour for the past 3 hours, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen bleeding or anticoagulation injury.

questions.takeaway: In sepsis, urine output < 0.5 mL/kg/hr for > 2 hours is an organ dysfunction criterion under Sepsis-3. Prioritize signs of end-organ perfusion failure over subjective complaints or pending results.

visualRationale.title: Safety and Infection Control / Prioritization
visualRationale.caption: In sepsis, urine output < 0.5 mL/kg/hr for > 2 hours is an organ dysfunction criterion under Sepsis-3. Prioritize signs of end-organ perfusion failure over subjective complaints or pending results.
visualRationale.conclusion: In sepsis, urine output < 0.5 mL/kg/hr for > 2 hours is an organ dysfunction criterion under Sepsis-3. Prioritize signs of end-organ perfusion failure over subjective complaints or pending results.

questions.stem: A clinic nurse suspects a patient has active tuberculosis based on symptoms and a positive QuantiFERON test. The patient has not yet been confirmed by sputum culture. What is the nurse's legal obligation regarding reporting?

options.text: Wait for confirmed sputum culture before reporting to the health department
Report only if the patient is homeless or a healthcare worker
Report the suspected case to the local public health department — TB is reportable on suspicion, not confirmation
The physician is solely responsible for reporting; nurses have no legal duty to report

questions.rationale: Mandatorily reportable diseases are reported on the basis of clinical suspicion, not confirmation. For TB and other high-priority communicable diseases, waiting for laboratory confirmation delays contact tracing and public health intervention. Reportable diseases vary by state but consistently include TB, STIs (gonorrhea, syphilis, chlamydia), meningococcal disease, hepatitis A/B/C, HIV, measles, pertussis, and foodborne illness outbreaks. Nurses have an independent legal duty to report regardless of physician action. The report goes to the local/state health department, which then initiates contact tracing.

distractorRationales.a: Wait for confirmed sputum culture before reporting to the health department misses the clinical pattern: Community Health priority points to Report the suspected case to the local public health department — TB is reportable on suspicion,, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen clinical safety risk.
distractorRationales.b: Report only if the patient is homeless or a healthcare worker misses the clinical pattern: Community Health priority points to Report the suspected case to the local public health department — TB is reportable on suspicion,, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen clinical safety risk.
distractorRationales.d: The physician is solely responsible for reporting; nurses have no legal duty to report misses the clinical pattern: Community Health priority points to Report the suspected case to the local public health department — TB is reportable on suspicion,, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen clinical safety risk.

questions.takeaway: Mandatory reportable diseases: report on suspicion (not confirmation); TB, STIs, meningitis, hepatitis — nurse has independent duty to report to public health; do not wait for lab confirmation.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the NCLEX review-batch library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: A patient with a legally designated healthcare proxy (durable power of attorney for healthcare) is brought to the ED unconscious after a large stroke. The healthcare proxy, who has legal authority, requests that all life-sustaining treatment be withdrawn. No written advance directive exists. What is the nurse's most appropriate action?

options.text: Proceed with withdrawal of life-sustaining treatment as legally directed by the healthcare proxy
Disregard the proxy's decision and provide full resuscitation until the patient can speak for themselves
Consult the ethics committee and continue full treatment until the committee renders a decision
Contact adult protective services, as a family member withdrawing treatment may constitute elder abuse

questions.rationale: A legally designated healthcare proxy (durable power of attorney for healthcare) has the legal authority to make medical decisions on behalf of an incapacitated patient. The proxy's role is substituted judgment — making the decision the patient would make if able. When a legally designated proxy with proper authority requests withdrawal of life-sustaining treatment, this request must be honored following institutional policies and applicable state law. The nurse should: verify the proxy's legal authority, document the decision, notify the physician immediately, involve the palliative care team, and provide comfort measures. An ethics consultation may be helpful if there is team conflict, but it is not required to delay a legally sound decision. This is not elder abuse — it is the proper exercise of legally granted decision-making authority.

distractorRationales.b: Disregard the proxy's decision and provide full resuscitation until the patient can speak for themselves misses the clinical pattern: Gerontology stroke points to Proceed with withdrawal of life-sustaining treatment as legally directed by the healthcare proxy, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen neurologic deterioration.
distractorRationales.c: Consult the ethics committee and continue full treatment until the committee renders a decision misses the clinical pattern: Gerontology stroke points to Proceed with withdrawal of life-sustaining treatment as legally directed by the healthcare proxy, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen neurologic deterioration.
distractorRationales.d: Contact adult protective services, as a family member withdrawing treatment may constitute elder abuse misses the clinical pattern: Gerontology stroke points to Proceed with withdrawal of life-sustaining treatment as legally directed by the healthcare proxy, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen neurologic deterioration.

questions.takeaway: Healthcare proxy with legal authority speaks for the incapacitated patient. Their decision must be respected and acted upon. No written advance directive is required when a valid proxy is designated.

questions.stem: An RN delegates to an LPN/LVN. Which task is within the LPN's scope of practice in most states?

options.text: Perform the initial nursing admission assessment for a newly admitted patient
Administer oral, IM, and subcutaneous medications and monitor response
Develop and update the nursing care plan based on patient assessment findings
Interpret cardiac rhythm strips and adjust IV vasopressor infusions per protocol

questions.rationale: LPN/LVN scope of practice typically includes: administering medications (oral, IM, SC, topical), performing basic wound care, collecting specimens, and monitoring and reporting patient status. Initial assessments (A) require RN-level nursing judgment and are an RN responsibility. Care plan development (C) is an RN function. Interpreting arrhythmias and adjusting vasopressors (D) requires advanced critical care RN knowledge. The key distinction is that LPNs implement care under RN supervision; RNs assess, plan, evaluate, and teach.

distractorRationales.a: Perform the initial nursing admission assessment for a newly admitted patient misses the clinical pattern: Delegation wound points to Administer oral, IM, and subcutaneous medications and monitor response, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen infection progression.
distractorRationales.c: Develop and update the nursing care plan based on patient assessment findings misses the clinical pattern: Delegation wound points to Administer oral, IM, and subcutaneous medications and monitor response, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen infection progression.
distractorRationales.d: Interpret cardiac rhythm strips and adjust IV vasopressor infusions per protocol misses the clinical pattern: Delegation wound points to Administer oral, IM, and subcutaneous medications and monitor response, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen infection progression.

questions.takeaway: LPN scope: administer medications, perform basic wound care, collect data, implement established care — initial assessment, care planning, and teaching are RN responsibilities.

questions.stem: A 24-year-old on haloperidol develops sudden onset torticollis (involuntary neck twisting), oculogyric crisis (eyes fixed upward), and severe muscle rigidity within 3 days of starting the medication. Which type of extrapyramidal symptom (EPS) is this, and what is the antidote?

options.text: Tardive dyskinesia; no effective antidote, may require medication discontinuation
Akathisia; treat with propranolol or benzodiazepines
Acute dystonia; treat with IV/IM diphenhydramine or benztropine
Pseudoparkinsonism; treat with amantadine or reduce antipsychotic dose

questions.rationale: Acute dystonia presents within hours to days of starting a high-potency antipsychotic (like haloperidol) as sudden, painful muscle spasms — torticollis, oculogyric crisis, opisthotonus. Treatment is IV or IM diphenhydramine (Benadryl) or benztropine (Cogentin), which are anticholinergic agents. Tardive dyskinesia (A) develops after months-years of use as involuntary repetitive movements (lip smacking, tongue protrusion). Akathisia (B) is inner restlessness and urge to move. Pseudoparkinsonism (D) mimics Parkinson's disease with tremor, rigidity, bradykinesia.

distractorRationales.a: Tardive dyskinesia; no effective antidote, may require medication discontinuation misses the clinical pattern: Antipsychotic Medication Safety rigidity points to Acute dystonia; treat with IV/IM diphenhydramine or benztropine, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen medication toxicity.
distractorRationales.b: Akathisia; treat with propranolol or benzodiazepines misses the clinical pattern: Antipsychotic Medication Safety rigidity points to Acute dystonia; treat with IV/IM diphenhydramine or benztropine, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen medication toxicity.
distractorRationales.d: Pseudoparkinsonism; treat with amantadine or reduce antipsychotic dose misses the clinical pattern: Antipsychotic Medication Safety rigidity points to Acute dystonia; treat with IV/IM diphenhydramine or benztropine, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen medication toxicity.

questions.takeaway: Acute dystonia (torticollis, oculogyric crisis) within days of antipsychotic = IV/IM diphenhydramine or benztropine (anticholinergics).
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the NCLEX review-batch library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: Parents call the pediatric triage nurse stating their 2-year-old has been having a seizure for 1 minute. The child has a temperature of 39.2°C (102.6°F) and no prior seizure history. What is the most important teaching point the nurse should emphasize?

options.text: Administer rectal diazepam immediately regardless of seizure duration
Most simple febrile seizures resolve within 5 minutes; call 911 if the seizure lasts more than 5 minutes or the child stops breathing
Put fingers in the mouth to prevent tongue-swallowing during the seizure
Administer acetaminophen immediately to stop the seizure

questions.rationale: Simple febrile seizures are benign, typically last <5 minutes, and are self-limiting. Parent teaching: (1) Do not restrain the child; (2) Turn child on side to prevent aspiration; (3) Do not insert anything in the mouth — risk of injury, not tongue-swallowing; (4) Time the seizure; (5) Call 911 if seizure >5 minutes (status epilepticus risk), child stops breathing, or a second seizure occurs. Rectal diazepam is not administered routinely unless prescribed for a child with known prolonged febrile seizures. Antipyretics do not abort an ongoing seizure.

distractorRationales.a: Administer rectal diazepam immediately regardless of seizure duration misses the clinical pattern: Pediatric Nursing breathing points to Most simple febrile seizures resolve within 5 minutes; call 911 if the seizure lasts more than, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.c: Put fingers in the mouth to prevent tongue-swallowing during the seizure misses the clinical pattern: Pediatric Nursing breathing points to Most simple febrile seizures resolve within 5 minutes; call 911 if the seizure lasts more than, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.d: Administer acetaminophen immediately to stop the seizure misses the clinical pattern: Pediatric Nursing breathing points to Most simple febrile seizures resolve within 5 minutes; call 911 if the seizure lasts more than, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.

questions.takeaway: Febrile seizure parent teaching: most resolve in <5 min; position on side, do not restrain, do not put anything in mouth; call 911 if >5 min or breathing stops.

visualRationale.title: Pediatric Nursing
visualRationale.caption: Febrile seizure parent teaching: most resolve in <5 min; position on side, do not restrain, do not put anything in mouth; call 911 if >5 min or breathing stops.
visualRationale.conclusion: Febrile seizure parent teaching: most resolve in <5 min; position on side, do not restrain, do not put anything in mouth; call 911 if >5 min or breathing stops.

questions.stem: The surgical team is about to make the first incision. Per the WHO Surgical Safety Checklist, which action must occur at this exact moment, and who must participate?

options.text: The surgeon alone verbally confirms the surgical site using the patient's consent form
The circulating nurse confirms patient identity and procedure using the patient's wristband
The entire surgical team pauses and verbally confirms: patient identity, surgical site and side, planned procedure, positioning, antibiotic prophylaxis, and any anticipated critical concerns
The anesthesiologist confirms ETT placement with waveform capnography before the time-out begins

questions.rationale: The WHO Surgical Safety Checklist 'time-out' occurs IMMEDIATELY before skin incision and is a mandatory complete pause involving ALL team members: surgeon, anesthesia provider, scrub technician, and circulating nurse. Each member must actively participate — passive listening does not satisfy the requirement. Items confirmed during time-out: (1) Patient identity (name, DOB, MRN); (2) Surgical site and laterality (confirmed with visible site marking); (3) Planned procedure; (4) Correct patient positioning; (5) Antibiotic prophylaxis administered within the prior 60 minutes; (6) Relevant imaging displayed; (7) Anticipated critical steps, significant blood loss, or special equipment needs. The time-out is distinct from the 'sign-in' (before anesthesia induction) and 'sign-out' (before leaving the OR). Implementation has reduced wrong-site surgery, wrong-patient surgery, and serious complications by more than 50% globally.

distractorRationales.a: The surgeon alone verbally confirms the surgical site using the patient's consent form misses the clinical pattern: Perioperative Care incision points to The entire surgical team pauses and verbally confirms: patient identity, surgical site and side, planned procedure,, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen clinical safety risk.
distractorRationales.b: The circulating nurse confirms patient identity and procedure using the patient's wristband misses the clinical pattern: Perioperative Care incision points to The entire surgical team pauses and verbally confirms: patient identity, surgical site and side, planned procedure,, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen clinical safety risk.
distractorRationales.d: The anesthesiologist confirms ETT placement with waveform capnography before the time-out begins misses the clinical pattern: Perioperative Care incision points to The entire surgical team pauses and verbally confirms: patient identity, surgical site and side, planned procedure,, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen clinical safety risk.

questions.takeaway: Time-out: ALL team members pause immediately before incision to verbally confirm patient ID, site/side, procedure, positioning, antibiotic timing, and critical concerns. Individual confirmation is insufficient.

questions.stem: A patient is being discharged on prednisone 40 mg daily for 10 days following an acute COPD exacerbation. Which statement by the patient indicates that ADDITIONAL teaching is needed?

options.text: 'I should not stop this medication suddenly without talking to my doctor.'
'I can stop taking this medication early if I start feeling better.'
'I should monitor my blood sugar more closely while taking this medication.'
'I should take this medication in the morning with food.'

questions.rationale: Stopping corticosteroids abruptly or early is dangerous and incorrect. Even a 10-day course suppresses the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis and requires a tapering strategy if used longer, and should always be completed as prescribed for a short course. Early discontinuation risks adrenal insufficiency and relapse of the condition being treated. The patient should understand that the full course must be completed, and that stopping suddenly (in longer courses) can cause an adrenal crisis. Statement A is correct — do not stop without medical advice. Statement C is correct — steroids cause hyperglycemia. Statement D is correct — morning administration mimics cortisol's natural diurnal rhythm and reduces GI upset.

distractorRationales.a: 'I should not stop this medication suddenly without talking to my doctor.' misses the clinical pattern: Pharmacology / Corticosteroid Teaching copd points to 'I can stop taking this medication early if I start feeling better.', so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen unstable glucose physiology.
distractorRationales.c: 'I should monitor my blood sugar more closely while taking this medication.' misses the clinical pattern: Pharmacology / Corticosteroid Teaching copd points to 'I can stop taking this medication early if I start feeling better.', so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen unstable glucose physiology.
distractorRationales.d: 'I should take this medication in the morning with food.' misses the clinical pattern: Pharmacology / Corticosteroid Teaching copd points to 'I can stop taking this medication early if I start feeling better.', so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen unstable glucose physiology.

questions.takeaway: Corticosteroid teaching: complete the full course, do not stop early or abruptly. Monitor blood glucose. Take in morning with food. Know signs of infection (immunosuppression). Report mood changes, edema, GI symptoms.

questions.stem: A patient on warfarin therapy for atrial fibrillation has an INR of 4.8 (therapeutic range 2.0-3.0). The patient has no active bleeding. What is the MOST appropriate nursing action?

options.text: Administer vitamin K 10 mg IV push immediately
Hold the warfarin dose, notify the provider, and monitor closely for bleeding signs
Administer fresh frozen plasma to rapidly reverse anticoagulation
Continue warfarin at the same dose and recheck INR in 3 days

questions.rationale: An INR of 4.8 represents supratherapeutic anticoagulation with elevated bleeding risk, but no active bleeding is present. Standard management for INR 4-10 without bleeding in a patient on warfarin for non-mechanical valve indication: hold warfarin, monitor for bleeding signs, and the INR will self-correct over 24-72 hours as the drug is metabolized. The provider should be notified to evaluate for dose adjustment. Vitamin K is indicated for INR > 10, or INR 4-10 with bleeding risk factors or active bleeding. High-dose IV vitamin K (10 mg) causes prolonged warfarin resistance and is reserved for emergencies. FFP is for life-threatening bleeding requiring immediate reversal. Continuing the same dose ignores the supratherapeutic level.

distractorRationales.a: Administer vitamin K 10 mg IV push immediately misses the clinical pattern: Pharmacology / Warfarin Safety bleeding points to Hold the warfarin dose, notify the provider, and monitor closely for bleeding signs, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen bleeding or anticoagulation injury.
distractorRationales.c: Administer fresh frozen plasma to rapidly reverse anticoagulation misses the clinical pattern: Pharmacology / Warfarin Safety bleeding points to Hold the warfarin dose, notify the provider, and monitor closely for bleeding signs, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen bleeding or anticoagulation injury.
distractorRationales.d: Continue warfarin at the same dose and recheck INR in 3 days misses the clinical pattern: Pharmacology / Warfarin Safety bleeding points to Hold the warfarin dose, notify the provider, and monitor closely for bleeding signs, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen bleeding or anticoagulation injury.

questions.takeaway: Supratherapeutic INR (no bleeding): INR 4-10 → hold warfarin + notify provider + monitor. INR > 10 or any bleeding → vitamin K. Life-threatening bleeding → FFP or 4-factor PCC (prothrombin complex concentrate). Never continue the same dose if INR is out of range.
